notes.anchor: I had been waiting eagerly for the letter-carriers
notes.note: The writer is the younger Marcus Cicero, then a student at Athens (44 BC); the letter-carriers had taken forty-six days to reach him from Italy. The 'kind, dearest father' is the orator himself.
Young Marcus had a reputation for dissipation; here he assures Tiro he has reformed and asks Tiro to be the 'trumpeter' of his improving good name to his father.

notes.anchor: with Cratippus I am bound not as a pupil to his teacher but as a son to his father
notes.note: Cratippus of Pergamum, the leading Peripatetic philosopher of the day, under whom Marcus studied at Athens; Cicero the elder admired him and dedicated De Officiis to his son partly on his account.

notes.anchor: what shall I say of Bruttius?
notes.note: Bruttius was Marcus's companion and Latin-declamation partner at Athens, living frugally near him; he is plausibly, though not certainly, the equestrian L. Bruttius known from Cicero's recommendations.

notes.anchor: I have started to declaim in Greek with Cassius
notes.note: A rhetorician named Cassius at Athens with whom Marcus practised Greek declamation; not the tyrannicide C. Cassius Longinus.

notes.anchor: the friends Cratippus brought with him from Mytilene
notes.note: Mytilene, the chief city of Lesbos, a noted seat of philosophy and rhetoric; Cratippus had taught there before coming to Athens and brought learned associates from it.

notes.anchor: Epicrates, too, the leading man of Athens
notes.note: Epicrates, a prominent Athenian citizen of Marcus's acquaintance, mentioned alongside Leonides as part of his social circle there. Otherwise unknown.

notes.anchor: what you write me about Gorgias
notes.note: Gorgias was a rhetoric tutor useful for daily declamation, but Cicero the elder had written 'in so many words' (diarrheden) that Marcus dismiss him at once, apparently as a bad influence; the obedient son complied.

notes.anchor: have a copyist sent to me as quickly as possible, a Greek one above all
notes.note: A librarius (copyist), preferably one trained in Greek, was needed to transcribe Marcus's hypomnemata (lecture-notebooks); the request shows the practical infrastructure of a student's study abroad.
Anteros, evidently a slave or freedman of the household whom young Marcus commends to Tiro's care, probably the bearer of the letter.
